description: Provide real-time, eventually-consistent document synchronization for SignalApp using SyncEngine and SyncManager so peers converge on a consistent DocumentSnapshot across peers.
Support multi-modal summarization: fast local summarization via LocalSummarizer.summarize and larger/remote models via RemoteSummarizer.summarize while preserving correctness and latency SLAs.
Offer an extensible search and indexing subsystem (InvertedIndex, Indexer, IndexWorker, GraphBuilder) that keeps IndexStats and SearchResultSnapshot up-to-date with new DocumentChange events.
Maintain presence and collaboration metadata through PresenceTracker, PeerPresence and PresenceStatus so the UI and sync logic can make correct availability decisions.

Enable plugin extensibility (Plugin, PluginHost, PluginContext, ExportPlugin, SearchPlugin) without breaking core invariants like data integrity and authenticated access (AppConfig + Authentication required).
Ensure robust offline-first behavior: offline edits are queued (OfflineSyncQueue, SyncQueue) and reconciled using ConflictCandidate, ConflictResolution and VectorClock semantics.
Monolithic 'signal-app' module boundary at app/ groups services (DocumentStore, SnapshotStore, SyncManager) and contracts—intended single module where most domain logic resides.
Dual summarizer strategy: LocalSummarizer is pure and synchronous-friendly; RemoteSummarizer encapsulates IO and longer-running async calls, separating concerns for latency and failure handling.

Event-driven storage layer: StorageEventBusContract/StorageEventBus and typed events (StorageEventCreated/Updated/Deleted/Linked) are the primary mechanism to propagate changes to indexing, snapshots and plugins.
Worker-pool indexing: IndexerContract + IndexWorker + WorkerPoolOptions implies asynchronous background indexing with separate IndexStats reporting to avoid blocking user writes.
Decoupled snapshot service: DocumentSnapshotService and SnapshotStore abstract snapshot creation and retrieval from the authoritative DocumentStore to support read-optimized queries and versioning.
Sync queue and session tracking: SyncQueue, OfflineSyncQueue, SyncSessionTracker and SyncSessionState implement ordered delivery and session hygiene to reconcile peer edits and acks (SyncAck).

LocalSummarizer.summarize is implemented as a pure function (no detected side effects) while RemoteSummarizer.summarize is marked as having IO/async side effects — callers must handle both deterministically.

There are duplicated/parallel service nodes: DocumentStore and DeprecatedDocumentStore exist concurrently, suggesting a migration in progress and potential code paths still referencing the deprecated API.

Presence logic was refactored to use PluginContext for document access (refactor note), indicating plugins now mediate some access that was previously direct (risk of permission or latency regressions).

A large set of contracts (e.g., IndexerContract, StorageEventBusContract, SyncQueueOptions) is defined but several corresponding concrete implementations are either duplicated or not apparent in the topology—possible gaps between contract and implementation.

Sync and snapshot-related services appear twice in the topology report (two SyncEngine/SyncManager and two DocumentStore/SnapshotStore entries), which likely reflects multiple instantiations or stale analysis artifacts that could hide coupling issues.

Constraints include Authentication required and Test coverage required, but there is no explicit authentication service node in the primary topology and Testing adoption is an undecided node—authentication and tests may be incomplete.
Observability is listed as a constraint, however there is no dedicated Observability service node (metrics/tracing/logging) surfaced; IndexStats exists but cross-cutting telemetry is not visible.

Offline sync primitives (OfflineSyncQueue, SyncQueue) and conflict primitives (ConflictCandidate, ConflictResolution, VectorClock) exist, but the presence of DeprecatedDocumentChange indicates legacy change formats may still be produced or consumed.

TypeScript adoption and Testing adoption are explicit decision nodes—TypeScript appears largely adopted (many contract/symbol nodes), but Testing adoption remains a decision indicating tests may be incomplete or uneven.
All document mutations must be authenticated and authorized (Authentication required) before being accepted into DocumentStore or SnapshotStore to prevent unauthorized edits.

Background indexing and summarization must be treated as async jobs (Async job processing): IndexWorker and RemoteSummarizer calls must be resilient and backpressure-aware to avoid blocking user commits.

Persistent storage invariants: DocumentStore, SnapshotStore and VersionHistory must persist authoritative DocumentSnapshot and VectorClock state so sync/merge decisions are deterministic after restart.
Observability: critical flows (SyncEngine, Indexer, RemoteSummarizer) must emit metrics and structured events (latency, failures, queue depth) to make regressions diagnosable.

Minimum automated test coverage (unit + integration) is required for contract-critical components (SyncManager, ConflictResolution, OfflineSyncQueue, Indexer) to avoid regression and reduce reliance on manual QA.

RemoteSummarizer.summarize will be a frequent source of latency spikes and transient errors under load; without standardized retries and timeouts this will cause user-facing stalls in summary-dependent features.
Flows still using DeprecatedDocumentStore will diverge from DocumentStore state and produce hard-to-reproduce consistency bugs during merges, especially when OfflineSyncQueue replays edits.

Indexer worker-pool sizing (IndexWorker + WorkerPoolOptions) is likely under-provisioned causing indexing lag and stale SearchResultSnapshots; search quality will degrade as write throughput increases.

PresenceTracker refactor to PluginContext removed direct document access; plugins without proper permission checks will either fail silently or leak access if PluginContext boundaries are not enforced.
Lack of centralized observability will make diagnosing sync conflicts and vector clock regressions (VectorClock, ConflictRecord) take significantly longer, increasing mean time to resolution.

TypeScript adoption and the presence of many Contract nodes suggests strong typing intent, yet legacy artifacts like DeprecatedDocumentStore and DeprecatedDocumentChange contradict a clean migration path.

The system aims for real-time convergence (SyncEngine/SyncManager) while also supporting offline-first replay (OfflineSyncQueue), introducing conflicting requirements for ordering guarantees, latency and resource usage.
Plugin-driven access (PluginHost, PluginContext) increases extensibility but conflicts with strict authentication/authorization constraints unless plugins are strictly sandboxed and audited.

Goals to require test coverage and observability are at odds with duplicated/unclear implementations (multiple SyncEngine/DocumentStore entries) which make writing deterministic tests and instrumentation more difficult.
